title: Pasta al burro e salvia
subtitle: Fettuccine with brown butter and crisp sage
excerpt: Fettuccine tossed in butter cooked until nutty and brown, with sage leaves fried until crisp and fragrant. One of the oldest and most austere of Italian pasta preparations — nothing between the pasta and the butter but time and heat.
sourceNotes: Based on the northern Italian burro e salvia tradition as documented in Marcella Hazan, 'Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking' (1992) and Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Italy' (2006). The combination of butter and sage is one of the foundational flavour pairings of the Po Valley and appears across pasta, risotto, and gnocchi preparations.

content.text: Burro e salvia is about two things: the flavour of brown butter and the texture of a crisp sage leaf. Brown butter, butter cooked past melting until the milk solids turn golden and nutty, is one of the most efficient flavour transformations in cooking. It takes ninety seconds longer than melted butter and tastes completely different. The sage fries in the browning butter at the same time, its moisture driven off, becoming brittle and intensely savoury.

The pasta must be well-salted, well-drained but not rinsed, and moved directly from the pot to the butter. The small amount of pasta water clinging to the pasta emulsifies with the butter into a light coating sauce. Parmesan is added at the table, not mixed through — it would make the sauce gluey.

items.prepNote: or tagliatelle; fresh pasta halves the cooking time

content.text: Cook {{fettuccine}} of fettuccine in a large pot of well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a cup of pasta water before draining.

While the pasta cooks, melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{sage}} sage leaves in a single layer. Cook, swirling the pan, until the butter turns golden-brown and smells nutty and the sage leaves are crisp — about 3–4 minutes. Watch carefully: the butter goes from brown to burnt quickly. If it starts to smell acrid, remove immediately from the heat.

Add the drained pasta to the brown butter pan over a low heat. Toss to coat, adding a splash of reserved pasta water if the butter looks dry. Divide between warmed bowls. Serve with {{parmesan}} grated parmesan and {{black-pepper-ground}} black pepper.

items.cause: Heat too high or pan left unattended
items.fix: Use a medium rather than high heat and watch constantly from the moment the butter melts. As soon as it smells nutty and turns golden, it is done.

items.symptom: Sage leaves are soft and oily, not crisp
items.cause: Not enough heat, or too many leaves crowding the pan
items.fix: The leaves need direct contact with the hot butter in a single layer. Work in two batches if necessary.

content.text: Butter and sage together define the cooking of Emilia-Romagna and Lombardy. The combination appears wherever fresh egg pasta is made: over tortellini, alongside gnocchi, under a roast. As a pasta dressing in its own right, it is the reference preparation for demonstrating good pasta without distraction — the kind of dish that appears in cooking schools because it requires technical understanding to get right, despite having only three ingredients.
